content.text: A decision tree turns an open-ended classification question into a deterministic sequence of yes/no checks. The on-call engineer works through the tree from the top in order. The first branch that resolves to YES terminates the tree and sets both the incident type and the minimum severity.

The tree enforces the most important rule of Claude incident management: safety and privacy checks always run first, before any quantitative measurement. An error rate of 0 % does not prove the incident is minor — it might be a silent safety or data breach.

attrs.alt: Decision tree flowchart: safety check -> error rate -> latency -> infra -> quality -> P3/P4

content.text: Start at the root (Alert Fires) and evaluate each diamond question in order. Do not skip questions even if an earlier answer seems obvious.
As soon as a question resolves to YES, follow the YES branch to the outcome node. Do not continue evaluating lower questions.
Outcome nodes set type and severity.
Each leaf sets both the incident type (e.g.
) and the minimum severity (e.g. P1). Severity can only be upgraded from there; the leaf value is a floor, not a ceiling.
Type can escalate; it cannot degrade.
As investigation reveals new evidence, re-enter the tree with the updated signals. If the re-classification yields a higher-consequence type, update the ticket immediately.
If all six questions resolve to NO, the incident is P3 or P4. Assign P3 if any customer has reported an issue; assign P4 for purely internal anomalies with no customer impact.
This step must run before any metric lookup. It covers the two categories that are always P0 regardless of quantitative signals.
Has the safety pipeline flagged a response in the affected time window?
Has a red-team probe succeeded when it previously failed?
Has the cross-session context probe returned positive?
Has the PII detection rule matched data in a response for a different customer?
If the answer to any of these is YES, classify as the matching safety or data/privacy sub-type and stop. Page all-hands. Do not wait for metric confirmation.

def step1_safety_privacy(signals) -> str | None:
    if signals.safety_pipeline_flag:  return "safety/policy-violation"
    if signals.guardrail_probe_fail:  return "safety/guardrail-bypass"
    if signals.injection_detected:    return "safety/prompt-injection"
    if signals.session_bleed:         return "data/session-bleed"
    if signals.pii_leak:              return "data/pii-leakage"
    if signals.data_pipeline_leak:    return "data/pipeline-failure"
    return None   # proceed to step 2
Pull the 5-minute error-rate gauge from Prometheus. The
clause on the alert rule means this value is already sustained — do not apply an additional waiting period manually.
If the error rate is between 5 % and 25 %, check the customer scope immediately: if > 20 % of organisations are affected the correct type is
, even though the rate is below 25 %.

Compare the current P50 latency against the 7-day rolling baseline for the same clock hour. Use a ratio rather than an absolute value — absolute thresholds break when traffic patterns shift (overnight vs. peak traffic have different natural latencies).

# Latency ratio: current P50 / 7-day same-hour baseline
histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(claude_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
/ clamp_min(claude_latency_baseline_p50, 0.001)

Check the serving layer and upstream dependencies before attributing symptoms to the model. Infrastructure failures often masquerade as availability incidents but require a different remediation team.
Is the model serving replica count below the configured minimum? Check
Is the dependency error rate above 5 % for any upstream service? Check the
Is the queue reject rate above 1 %? Check
If any of these is YES, classify as the matching infrastructure sub-type. The remediation path (replica restart, dependency failover, capacity scaling) differs from a model or availability fix.

By this point the standard availability and infrastructure signals are green. The incident may be a silent quality regression — the model is returning 200 OK but producing degraded output. This step requires the quality-scoring pipeline to be running continuously; without it this step cannot execute.

# Quality drop fraction vs 7-day baseline
1 - (
  claude_completion_quality_score
  / clamp_min(claude_quality_baseline_7d, 0.01)
)
If the quality pipeline is not running, mark this step as
and proceed to P3 — do not assume the quality is fine just because you cannot measure it. File a follow-up ticket to deploy the probe.
All quantitative signals are within normal range. The incident is either P3 or P4.
At least one customer has reported an issue
Real impact exists even if metrics don't show it yet
A monitoring anomaly was detected but no customer impact
All signals normal and no reports
Likely a spurious alert; add suppression if it recurs
The full tree can be run as a function. The result is a namedtuple with both the incident type and the floor severity, ready to be written into the PagerDuty incident and the Slack bridge message.
Write the classification to the incident ticket within 5 minutes of the alert firing. The minimum required fields are:
field is critical for post-mortem calibration: if incidents consistently resolve at step 2 with step-5 root causes, the quality pipeline is not surfacing issues fast enough.
These are the most frequent classification mistakes observed in post-mortems. Understanding them helps catch errors before they compound.
Skipping Step 1 because error rate is 0 %
Engineer saw no errors and went straight to Step 3. Safety pipeline had flagged a violation 4 minutes earlier.
Step 1 must always run first, unconditionally. A zero error rate does not clear safety signals.
Classifying infra failure as availability outage
High error rate was attributed to an outage. Root cause was auth dependency returning wrong tokens — a data incident.
Run Step 4 before concluding P0 outage. Dependency errors may mask a data incident.
Under-classifying a quality issue as P4
No customer complaints, no errors. Quality probe was not running so Step 5 returned unknown. Classified P4.
Mark Step 5 as unknown, not green. Escalate to P3 and deploy the quality probe immediately.
Treating a jailbreak as a P2 technical issue
Guardrail probe success rate dropped 2 %. Engineer read it as a minor regression.
Any guardrail bypass is safety/guardrail-bypass P0. Percentage is irrelevant — even 1 bypass is a breach.
Staying at initial type after new evidence
Latency regression classified P1. Investigation found corrupted model weights — a model-quality incident.
Re-run the tree when root cause changes. Update ticket type and escalate appropriately.

The thresholds in the tree (> 25 %, > 2x, > 5 % for dependencies) are defaults calibrated for a typical production Claude deployment. They should be reviewed quarterly and adjusted when traffic patterns change significantly.
Customer complaints consistently arrive before the P1 threshold fires — lower the Step 2 P1 boundary
A model variant with naturally higher latency causes false-positive P1 alerts — add a per-variant baseline
A new enterprise customer with strict SLAs requires faster classification — create a tenant-scoped tree
Overnight traffic produces frequent P3 alerts with no customer impact — raise the P3 floor
A high-volume batch integration causes 1-2 % error spikes that always self-resolve — raise the P2 entry
On-call fatigue is increasing — audit alert volume and raise the least-actionable thresholds first

# threshold_config.py — centralise tree thresholds for easy calibration
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TreeThresholds:
    error_rate_p0:            float = 0.25
    error_rate_p1:            float = 0.05
    error_rate_p2:            float = 0.01
    customer_scope_p0:        float = 0.20
    customer_scope_p1:        float = 0.05
    latency_ratio_p0:         float = 5.0
    latency_ratio_p1:         float = 2.0
    latency_ratio_p2:         float = 1.5
    dependency_error_pct_p1:  float = 5.0
    queue_reject_rate_p1:     float = 0.01
    quality_drop_p1:          float = 0.10
    quality_drop_p2:          float = 0.05

# Override for tenants with tighter SLAs
ENTERPRISE_THRESHOLDS = TreeThresholds(
    error_rate_p1=0.02,         # 2 % triggers P1 instead of 5 %
    latency_ratio_p1=1.5,       # 1.5x triggers P1 instead of 2x
)
Integrating the Tree with Your Alerting Stack
The decision tree is most useful when it runs automatically on each alert and pre-fills the incident ticket. Here is a minimal integration pattern for a PagerDuty webhook handler:

The six-step decision tree provides a deterministic path from alert to typed, severity-floored classification in under 5 minutes. Safety and privacy checks always run first. Each subsequent step narrows the scope from availability to latency to infrastructure to model quality. A full NO sweep lands at P3/P4.
Step 1 (safety/privacy) is the only step that can produce P0 from a qualitative signal alone
First YES terminates the tree — do not continue evaluating lower questions
run_decision_tree() returns type, floor severity, and the step that resolved it
Record classification within 5 minutes; include the step and the metric value that triggered it
Re-enter the tree if new evidence arrives — type escalates, never degrades
Consistent resolution at step 2 when the root cause is step 5 means the quality probe is too slow
